_meta.authoringNotes: Medical content reviewed against published hematology literature, the 2017 WHO hypereosinophilic syndrome classification criteria, and established guidelines on eosinophil disorders.

h1Title: Eosinophilia: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Eosinophilia is an elevation in the peripheral blood eosinophil count above the normal upper limit of 500 cells per microliter (cells/mcL), reflecting an underlying disorder that triggers eosinophil production and survival. Eosinophils are granulocytic white blood cells that reside primarily in tissues (particularly the gastrointestinal mucosa, lungs, and skin) where they mediate defense against parasites and modulate allergic inflammation. When eosinophilia is mild (500-1,500 cells/mcL), the cause is most commonly allergic conditions (asthma, drug reactions, skin disease) or tissue-invasive helminthic parasites. When eosinophilia is marked (above 1,500 cells/mcL) and persistent (longer than 6 months) without an identifiable secondary cause, hypereosinophilic syndrome (HES) is considered — a clonal or idiopathic disorder of eosinophil overproduction that carries risk of end-organ damage to the heart (fibrosis, thrombosis), nervous system (neuropathy), and GI tract (EoE, eosinophilic gastroenteritis).

keyStats.prevalence: Eosinophilia is common — present in 4-8% of routine blood counts in general clinical practice; marked eosinophilia (above 1,500 cells/mcL) is much rarer, occurring in approximately 0.1% of hospitalizations; hypereosinophilic syndrome has an estimated incidence of 0.5-6 per 100,000 per year
keyStats.demographics: Mild eosinophilia is equally common in men and women and across all ages; HES has a male predominance (approximately 4:1), though this may partly reflect referral bias; allergic eosinophilia is more common in atopic individuals
keyStats.avgAge: Secondary eosinophilia (allergic or parasitic) presents across the lifespan; HES typically presents in adults aged 20-50 years, with a peak in the third and fourth decades
keyStats.globalCases: Parasitic helminth infections are the leading cause of eosinophilia in tropical and subtropical regions — Strongyloides stercoralis, hookworm, Ascaris, and filarial infections produce marked eosinophilia in millions of people globally; in high-income countries, allergic causes dominate

definition: Eosinophilia (ICD-10: D72.1) is defined as a peripheral blood absolute eosinophil count (AEC) above 500 cells/mcL (0.5 x 10^9/L). The classification of eosinophilia by severity is: mild (500-1,500 cells/mcL), moderate (1,500-5,000 cells/mcL), marked (above 5,000 cells/mcL). Eosinophils originate from CD34+ hematopoietic progenitor cells in the bone marrow under the influence of interleukin-5 (IL-5), granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor (GM-CSF), and interleukin-3 (IL-3). Once produced, eosinophils circulate briefly (approximately 18 hours) before migrating into tissues where they survive for days to weeks, releasing toxic granule proteins (major basic protein, eosinophil cationic protein, eosinophil peroxidase) that damage parasites and host tissues alike. The causes of eosinophilia are broadly divided into secondary (reactive) eosinophilia driven by external triggers (allergens, parasites, drugs, autoimmune inflammation) and primary (clonal or neoplastic) eosinophilia driven by intrinsic bone marrow defects (myeloproliferative neoplasms with PDGFR or FGFR rearrangements, chronic eosinophilic leukemia). The distinction is critical because secondary eosinophilia is treated by managing the underlying cause, while primary eosinophilia may require corticosteroids, monoclonal antibodies (mepolizumab), or tyrosine kinase inhibitors (imatinib for FIP1L1-PDGFRA).

typesClassification.description: The most common form in clinical practice — mild eosinophilia is present in 4-8% of routine blood counts in high-income countries. The most common causes are allergic conditions (asthma, allergic rhinitis, drug reactions, skin allergies), helminth infections (in endemic areas), and autoimmune diseases. Most cases require no specific treatment — management of the underlying cause is sufficient.

Less common — moderate eosinophilia narrows the differential diagnosis significantly. At this level, parasitic infections, drug reactions (DRESS syndrome, drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms), and early HES become prominent. Persistent moderate eosinophilia without identifiable cause warrants hematology evaluation.

Marked eosinophilia strongly suggests HES, a helminth parasitic infection, or hematologic malignancy. At this level, the toxic effects of eosinophil granule proteins on end organs become a clinical concern — myocardial toxicity (eosinophil infiltration causing necrosis and fibrosis), neurological involvement, and GI complications are the primary risks.

Defined as AEC above 1,500 cells/mcL on two occasions at least 1 month apart with end-organ damage attributable to eosinophilia and no identifiable secondary cause. HES is classified into: myeloid/lymphoproliferative HES (clonal, driven by PDGFR/FGFR mutations, responds to imatinib), lymphoid HES (driven by abnormal T-cell clones producing IL-5, may respond to anti-IL-5 therapy), and overlap HES (limited to a single organ such as eosinophilic esophagitis or gastroenteritis). Approximately 50% of HES cases are idiopathic.

A clonal hematologic malignancy characterized by marked eosinophilia with evidence of myeloproliferative features — blast cells in blood or marrow, cytogenetic abnormalities, or molecular markers (FIP1L1-PDGFRA fusion). This entity is now classified under 'chronic eosinophilic leukemia, not otherwise specified (CEL-NOS)' and requires tyrosine kinase inhibitor therapy.

primarySymptoms: Skin manifestations: urticaria, angioedema, pruritic papules, or erythema multiforme — the skin is a major eosinophil tissue reservoir; eosinophil degranulation causes itching, hive-like lesions, and papular eruptions that may flare and remit; drug-induced eosinophilia often presents with skin involvement.

Pulmonary symptoms: cough, dyspnea, or wheezing — eosinophils accumulate in the bronchial mucosa in allergic and parasitic eosinophilia; Löffler syndrome (transient pulmonary eosinophilia) presents with cough, dyspnea, and fleeting chest X-ray infiltrates; chronic eosinophilic pneumonia causes progressive dyspnea.

GI symptoms: abdominal pain, diarrhea, dysphagia — eosinophils infiltrate the GI mucosa in eosinophilic gastroenteritis (causing abdominal pain, protein-losing enteropathy, and peripheral edema) and eosinophilic esophagitis (causing dysphagia and food impaction in younger patients).

Peripheral neuropathy: paresthesias, motor weakness, or mononeuritis multiplex — eosinophil-mediated nerve damage is a hallmark of neurological involvement in HES; the most common manifestation is peripheral neuropathy affecting the lower extremities asymmetrically.
Myalgia and arthralgia — myalgia from eosinophil-derived toxic proteins affecting muscle tissue; arthralgia is common in eosinophilic myositis and in HES; muscle enzymes (CK) are usually normal.
Fever and constitutional symptoms — chronic eosinophilia can cause low-grade fever, weight loss, and fatigue when eosinophil granule release is systemic (as in HES or DRESS syndrome).

Cardiac symptoms from eosinophilic myocarditis — the heart is a critical end-organ target in HES; eosinophilic infiltration of the myocardium causes chest pain, heart failure symptoms (dyspnea, edema), and arrhythmias; echocardiogram shows ventricular dysfunction or pericardial effusion.

Thromboembolic events from eosinophil-induced hypercoagulability — eosinophils release tissue factor and other procoagulant factors; HES patients have elevated risk of venous thromboembolism (DVT, PE) and arterial thrombosis (stroke, MI); anticoagulation may be required.
earlyWarningSigns: Persistent eosinophilia above 1,000 cells/mcL on two or more occasions — repeated eosinophilia that is not explained by an obvious allergic or infectious trigger warrants investigation for HES or occult parasitic infection
New-onset angioedema, urticaria, or pruritic rash in a patient with eosinophilia — consider hypereosinophilic syndrome or DRESS (drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms) syndrome
Cardiac symptoms (chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations) in a patient with marked eosinophilia — eosinophilic myocarditis is a life-threatening complication that may present subtly at first
Unexplained neuropathy or motor weakness in a patient with eosinophilia — eosinophil-mediated neuropathy is one of the organ complications of HES; neurology referral is urgent

Eosinophilia developing during or after a course of a new medication — DRESS syndrome (drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms) typically develops 2-8 weeks after starting a new drug and is a medical emergency
emergencySigns: Acute chest pain, dyspnea, or cardiac arrhythmia with marked eosinophilia — eosinophilic myocarditis can present with acute coronary syndrome, myocarditis, or pericarditis; echocardiogram and cardiac enzymes are urgently required
Stroke, TIA, or acute mononeuritis multiplex — eosinophil-mediated CNS vasculitis or thrombosis in HES can cause acute neurological events; MRI of the brain is urgent
Severe dysphagia or food impaction — eosinophilic esophagitis can cause acute food impaction requiring endoscopic removal
DRESS syndrome with organ involvement (liver failure, renal failure, cardiac involvement) — mortality of 10-20%; requires emergent hospitalization and corticosteroids
Severe anemia or pancytopenia from bone marrow involvement — rare, but can indicate eosinophilic leukemia or advanced HES with marrow infiltration

causes.cause: Allergic/atopic conditions (asthma, allergic rhinitis, drug allergy, atopic dermatitis)
causes.description: The most common cause of mild eosinophilia in high-income countries — allergen exposure triggers Th2 cytokine release (IL-4, IL-5, IL-13), driving eosinophil production and recruitment to tissues. Allergic eosinophilia is typically mild (500-1,500 cells/mcL), tissue-localized (skin, lung, nose), and does not cause end-organ damage. Peripheral eosinophilia may be absent if eosinophils are tissue-localized.

causes.cause: Helminth parasitic infections (Strongyloides stercoralis, hookworm, Ascaris, filaria)
causes.description: Tissue-invasive helminths are the most common cause of marked eosinophilia (1,500-5,000+ cells/mcL) in tropical and subtropical regions. Strongyloides stercoralis (threadworm) is particularly important — it causes marked eosinophilia, can persist for decades through autoinfection, and can cause hyperinfection syndrome in immunocompromised hosts. Hookworm and Ascaris cause mild-moderate eosinophilia. Filarial infections (Wuchereria bancrofti, Loa loa) cause marked eosinophilia with tropical distribution.

causes.cause: Drug hypersensitivity reactions (DRESS syndrome, other drug reactions)
causes.description: DRESS syndrome (Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms) is a severe, potentially life-threatening drug reaction — aromatic anticonvulsants (phenytoin, carbamazepine, phenobarbital), allopurinol, sulfonamides, and vancomycin are common triggers. DRESS presents with rash, fever, lymphadenopathy, hepatitis, and marked eosinophilia (often 1,000-5,000 cells/mcL). Mortality of 10-20% without treatment.

causes.cause: Autoimmune/rheumatologic diseases (eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis, EGPA)
causes.description: EGPA (formerly Churg-Strauss syndrome) is a systemic ANCA-associated vasculitis characterized by asthma, sinusitis, eosinophilia, and small vessel vasculitis affecting nerves, skin, and heart. Eosinophilia is a defining feature (present in 80% of patients). EGPA is a medical emergency when vasculitic complications (mononeuritis multiplex, glomerulonephritis, cardiac involvement) emerge.

causes.cause: Eosinophilic gastrointestinal disorders (eosinophilic esophagitis, eosinophilic gastroenteritis)
causes.description: EG and EoE are organ-restricted eosinophilic disorders — eosinophils infiltrate the GI tract mucosa, causing dysphagia (EoE), abdominal pain, protein loss, and GI bleeding. EoE is the leading cause of esophageal food impaction in children and young adults and is increasing in prevalence. The peripheral eosinophil count may be normal in organ-restricted disease.

HES is defined as AEC above 1,500 cells/mcL persisting longer than 6 months with end-organ damage and no identifiable secondary cause. Myeloproliferative HES (driven by PDGFR/FGFR mutations) and lymphoid HES (driven by IL-5-producing T-cell clones) are the two major clonal variants. Idiopathic HES is a diagnosis of exclusion.

Cortisol is a natural inhibitor of eosinophils — in adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease), the absence of cortisol allows eosinophils to rise significantly. Eosinophilia with a relative lymphopenia is a classic laboratory finding in acute adrenal crisis (the 'stress eosinophilia' pattern).

Some solid tumors (particularly adenocarcinomas of the lung, GI tract, and cervix) produce cytokines that drive reactive eosinophilia — paraneoplastic eosinophilia. Hematologic malignancies (lymphomas, chronic myeloid leukemia, mastocytosis) can also present with eosinophilia as a presenting feature.

riskFactors.description: A personal or family history of asthma, allergic rhinitis, atopic dermatitis, or food allergy indicates a Th2-polarized immune response that predisposes to eosinophilia from allergic triggers.

riskFactors.factor: Residence in or travel to tropical/endemic regions
riskFactors.description: Travel to or residence in tropical regions with endemic helminthic parasites (Southeast Asia, sub-Saharan Africa, Latin America) is the strongest risk factor for parasitic eosinophilia. Strongyloides and hookworm are globally distributed in tropical areas.

riskFactors.factor: Chronic use of certain medications (allopurinol, anticonvulsants, sulfonamides)
riskFactors.description: Aromatic anticonvulsants (phenytoin, carbamazepine, phenobarbital), allopurinol, sulfonamide antibiotics, and vancomycin are associated with DRESS syndrome — the most serious drug-induced eosinophilic reaction.

Immunocompromised patients — particularly those on corticosteroids or with HIV/AIDS — are at risk for Strongyloides hyperinfection syndrome, which causes massive eosinophilia and disseminated infection. Eosinophilia may be absent in profoundly immunosuppressed patients.
HES has a marked male predominance (approximately 4:1), reflecting the higher prevalence of PDGFR-associated myeloid HES in males. This sex ratio is not explained by exposure differences.

affectedDemographics: Mild eosinophilia affects males and females equally in the context of allergic disease
HES has a marked male predominance (4:1); the reasons include a higher prevalence of myeloproliferative PDGFR+ HES in males

Parasitic eosinophilia is distributed by geography — Strongyloides, hookworm, Ascaris, and filarial infections follow the geographic distribution of their vectors and intermediate hosts; immigrants and travelers to endemic areas are at risk
EGPA (eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis) has equal sex distribution; asthma (which typically precedes EGPA) is more common in females
EoE is increasingly recognized in children and young adults — 70% of patients are male
diagnosisOverview: The evaluation of eosinophilia follows a stepwise algorithm based on the severity and persistence of the elevated AEC. First, confirm that eosinophilia is real (repeat CBC) and exclude reactive causes of eosinophilia by history (allergies, medications, recent travel, rash). Mild, transient eosinophilia in a well-appearing patient with a clear allergic trigger requires no further workup. Persistent moderate-to-marked eosinophilia or eosinophilia with any organ symptoms requires a structured workup: (1) stool ova and parasite examination (three samples) to detect helminth infections including Strongyloides; (2) serology for Strongyloides stercoralis (more sensitive than stool exam); (3) ANCA testing if signs of vasculitis (EGPA) are present; (4) serum tryptase (elevated in mastocytosis and some HES variants); (5) IgE level (elevated in allergic and parasitic conditions); (6) echocardiogram to assess for eosinophilic cardiac involvement in any patient with AEC above 1,500 cells/mcL; (7) bone marrow examination with cytogenetics and molecular testing (FIP1L1-PDGFRA, PDGFRB, FGFR1, JAK2) when HES is suspected. Patients with unexplained moderate-to-marked eosinophilia persisting beyond 4-6 weeks despite negative parasitic workup should be referred to hematology.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the absolute eosinophil count (AEC = total WBC x % eosinophils / 100) and confirms persistent eosinophilia on repeat testing. Serial AEC measurements track eosinophilia burden and response to treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard EDTA blood draw. Results within 1 hour.

diagnosticTests.test: Stool ova and parasite examination (three separate samples)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects helminth eggs and larvae — particularly Strongyloides stercoralis, hookworm (Necator americanus, Ancylostoma duodenale), Ascaris lumbricoides, and Trichuris trichiura. Three samples collected on different days are required to maximize sensitivity (ova excretion is intermittent).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Three fresh stool samples (ideally collected within 2 hours or preserved in formalin); each sample examined by microscopy for ova and larvae. Results available same day to 2 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Serology for Strongyloides is more sensitive than stool examination — particularly for low-burden infection or when stool samples are not processed with the agar plate culture method. Essential for any patient with eosinophilia who has lived in or traveled to tropical regions.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw for Strongyloides serology; results from reference parasitology lab in 5-10 days. Serology cannot distinguish between past and current infection (antibodies persist); clinical correlation is required.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Tryptase is elevated in mastocytosis and in some HES variants (tryptase-high HES). A tryptase above 20 ng/mL suggests mast cell involvement and warrants evaluation for mastocytosis or PDGFR+ HES (which shares the same molecular driver as some mastocytosis cases).
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw; results from reference lab in 2-5 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The FIP1L1-PDGFRA fusion is the defining molecular abnormality of myeloid/lymphoproliferative HES (PDGFR+ HES) — a clonally driven HES that responds dramatically to imatinib. Testing is mandatory in any patient with HES or unexplained AEC above 1,500 cells/mcL.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood or bone marrow sample for FISH or PCR for FIP1L1-PDGFRA. Results from specialized hematology labs in 5-10 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Eosinophilic myocarditis and endomyocardial fibrosis are life-threatening complications of HES — the cardiac assessment is essential for any patient with AEC above 1,500 cells/mcL, even without symptoms. Echocardiogram looks for ventricular dysfunction, wall motion abnormalities, mural thrombus, and pericardial effusion.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Transthoracic echocardiogram, 30-45 minutes. Results available immediately.

diagnosticTests.test: Bone marrow examination with cytogenetics and molecular testing
diagnosticTests.purpose: Bone marrow aspirate and biopsy with cytogenetics (karyotype, FISH) and molecular testing (FIP1L1-PDGFRA, PDGFRB, FGFR1, JAK2, BCR-ABL) is indicated when HES is suspected — it distinguishes clonal HES (requiring targeted therapy) from reactive eosinophilia and from idiopathic HES. Shows characteristic eosinophil infiltration and may reveal mast cell infiltration if PDGFR+.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Bone marrow aspirate and biopsy from posterior iliac crest under sedation. Results in 5-10 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: ANCA testing is indicated when features of systemic vasculitis (EGPA) are present — EGPA is associated with MPO-ANCA (positive in approximately 40% of patients). History of asthma, sinusitis, neuropathy, and palpable purpura in a patient with eosinophilia is highly suggestive of EGPA.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw for ANCA (MPO and PR3). Results in 1-3 days.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of eosinophilia depends on the underlying cause and the degree of elevation and tissue involvement. Asymptomatic mild eosinophilia (absolute count below 1500 cells per microliter) without organ involvement may simply require monitoring and investigation of the cause. Corticosteroids are the first-line treatment for hypereosinophilic syndromes and eosinophilic organ infiltration: prednisone 0.5 to 1 mg per kg daily, with taper based on response and eosinophil count monitoring. For steroid-responsive hypereosinophilic syndrome, mepolizumab (anti-IL-5) is an effective steroid-sparing agent. For FIP1L1-PDGFRA-positive chronic eosinophilic leukemia, imatinib is the treatment of choice and induces rapid complete remission in most patients. Helminth infections causing eosinophilia are treated with albendazole or ivermectin as appropriate for the specific parasite. Allergic conditions driving eosinophilia (asthma, atopic dermatitis, eosinophilic esophagitis) respond to targeted therapies including anti-IL-5 agents (mepolizumab, reslizumab, benralizumab) and anti-IL-4R alpha dupilumab. Eosinophilic myocarditis requires urgent high-dose corticosteroid therapy and specialist management. Drug-induced eosinophilia resolves upon discontinuation of the causative agent. Serial monitoring of absolute eosinophil count guides treatment response across all eosinophilic conditions. Patient education about treatment adherence, monitoring for complications, and recognizing signs of disease progression is essential. Shared decision-making between the patient and treatment team improves outcomes and quality of life. Regular follow-up visits allow assessment of treatment response, management of adverse effects, and adjustment of therapy as needed. Multidisciplinary involvement including specialist physicians, nursing staff, social workers, and mental health professionals optimizes comprehensive care delivery.

medicalTreatments.name: Prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg/day orally
medicalTreatments.description: First-line treatment for most HES variants and eosinophilic organ involvement (EG, EoE, EGPA). Corticosteroids rapidly suppress eosinophil production and are highly effective — AEC typically falls within days. Long-term maintenance therapy (lowest effective dose, often every-other-day) is required for 6-12 months. Steroid-sparing agents (mepolizumab) are preferred for long-term management to avoid corticosteroid toxicity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: AEC normalizes in 80-90% of HES patients within 2-4 weeks of corticosteroid therapy; organ involvement (cardiac, neurologic) improves in most patients who receive early treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Mepolizumab (anti-IL-5, Nucala) 300 mg SC every 4 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: Monoclonal antibody targeting interleukin-5 (the primary cytokine driving eosinophil production and survival). Approved for severe eosinophilic asthma and EGPA, and used off-label for HES. Reduces eosinophil count and can maintain remission in patients who are corticosteroid-responsive. Allows reduction or discontinuation of corticosteroids.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces exacerbations and enables corticosteroid reduction in eosinophilic asthma; clinical data support efficacy in HES — 50-70% of HES patients achieve partial or complete response; FDA-approved for EGPA.

medicalTreatments.name: Imatinib 100-400 mg/day orally (for FIP1L1-PDGFRA+ HES)
medicalTreatments.description: Imatinib is a tyrosine kinase inhibitor that targets the PDGFRA fusion protein driving PDGFR+ HES. FIP1L1-PDGFRA+ HES is the most steroid-responsive HES variant — imatinib produces dramatic and sustained remission at doses far lower than those used for CML. Must be confirmed by molecular testing before use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Over 95% of FIP1L1-PDGFRA+ HES patients achieve complete hematologic and molecular remission on imatinib within 2-4 weeks; the response is durable for years. This is the most dramatic therapeutic response in hematology.

medicalTreatments.name: Albendazole 400 mg orally twice daily (for Strongyloides and helminths)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line antihelminthic for Strongyloides stercoralis, hookworm, Ascaris, and most intestinal nematodes. Ivermectin (200 mcg/kg/day orally for 2 days) is an equally effective alternative for Strongyloides and is better tolerated. For disseminated Strongyloides or hyperinfection syndrome, prolonged courses (7-14 days) are required, and some protocols add ivermectin.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Albendazole (single 400 mg dose or 400 mg bid x 3 days) clears Strongyloides in 80-90% of cases; a second dose at 2 weeks is often given for optimal cure rates. Eosinophilia normalizes within 2-4 weeks of successful treatment.

medicalTreatments.name: Ivermectin 200 mcg/kg/day orally for 1-2 days (for Strongyloides)
medicalTreatments.description: Ivermectin is the preferred treatment for Strongyloides stercoralis — better efficacy and tolerability than albendazole for this organism. For uncomplicated Strongyloides, a single 200 mcg/kg dose clears infection in most patients. For hyperinfection or disseminated Strongyloides, 200 mcg/kg daily for 7-14 days or longer is used.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rates of 90-95% for uncomplicated Strongyloides with single-dose ivermectin; higher with repeat dosing. Eosinophilia resolves within 2-4 weeks of clearing the infection.

medicalTreatments.name: Discontinuation of offending drug (DRESS syndrome)
medicalTreatments.description: The single most important intervention in DRESS syndrome — immediate discontinuation of the suspect drug. All aromatic anticonvulsants (phenytoin, carbamazepine, phenobarbital), allopurinol, sulfonamides, vancomycin, and dapsone are common culprits. The offending drug must be permanently avoided in the future.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Drug discontinuation alone is insufficient — systemic corticosteroids are required for organ involvement in DRESS; however, removing the trigger is essential to prevent progression and death.

surgicalOptions.name: Endoscopic dilation and food bolus removal (EoE)
surgicalOptions.description: Acute food impaction in eosinophilic esophagitis requires urgent endoscopy for food bolus removal. For stricturing from chronic EoE, endoscopic dilation (bougienage or balloon dilation) relieves dysphagia. Dilation does not treat the underlying eosinophilic inflammation — disease-directed therapy is also required.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Endoscopic food bolus removal is life-saving in acute impaction; endoscopic dilation relieves dysphagia in 75-90% of EoE patients with strictures, though repeated dilations may be needed.

alternativeTreatments.name: Interferon-alpha
alternativeTreatments.description: An alternative eosinophil-lowering agent for imatinib-refractory PDGFR+ HES and for idiopathic HES when corticosteroids and mepolizumab are insufficient. Administered as subcutaneous injections three times per week. Has significant side effects (flu-like syndrome, depression, cytopenias) that limit its use.

alternativeTreatments.name: Hydroxyurea
alternativeTreatments.description: A cytoreductive agent used in hematologic conditions — reduces eosinophil count by suppressing bone marrow production. Used as an adjunct in steroid-refractory HES when mepolizumab or imatinib are unavailable or contraindicated. Requires regular CBC monitoring for cytopenias.

whySeeSpecialist: Hematology referral is required for any patient with AEC above 1,500 cells/mcL persisting beyond 4-6 weeks despite negative parasitic and allergic workup, or with any end-organ involvement (cardiac, neurological, GI). This ensures access to bone marrow examination, FIP1L1-PDGFRA testing, and HES-specific therapies (imatinib, mepolizumab). Allergy/immunology referral is appropriate for allergic eosinophilia (asthma, EoE, allergic GI disease). Infectious disease referral is needed for parasitic eosinophilia in travelers or immigrants from endemic areas.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a hematologist with experience in eosinophilic disorders — ask whether they are familiar with HES classification, FIP1L1-PDGFRA testing, and imatinib-based HES treatment. For parasitic eosinophilia, an infectious disease specialist with tropical medicine expertise is essential. For EoE or EG (eosinophilic gastroenteritis), a gastroenterologist or allergist/immunologist familiar with eosinophilic GI disease is optimal.
preventionStrategies: Albendazole or ivermectin treatment for Strongyloides before starting immunosuppression (corticosteroids, chemotherapy) in patients with risk factors for helminth exposure — prevents hyperinfection syndrome, which is often fatal
Avoidance of known allergen triggers for patients with allergic eosinophilia — effective in reducing mild eosinophilia and preventing asthma exacerbations

Permanent avoidance of the culprit drug in patients who develop DRESS — aromatic anticonvulsants, allopurinol, sulfonamides, and vancomycin are the most common triggers; a complete list should be documented and shared with all future healthcare providers

Pre-travel counseling and post-travel eosinophilia screening for individuals traveling to endemic helminth regions — particularly important for immunocompromised travelers who will receive corticosteroids
lifestyleModifications: Avoid known allergen triggers if eosinophilia is driven by allergy — environmental control measures for asthma and allergic rhinitis (HEPA filters, allergen-impermeable bedding, regular vacuuming) reduce eosinophil activation
Inform all healthcare providers of any history of eosinophilia or HES — this alerts providers to consider eosinophilic complications in any subsequent illness and to avoid drugs associated with DRESS

Do not take medications associated with DRESS (aromatic anticonvulsants, allopurinol, sulfonamides) without careful physician monitoring — these drugs require vigilance for rash, fever, and eosinophilia
Consider wearing a medical alert bracelet if you have HES with cardiac or neurological involvement — ensures emergency providers are aware of your condition in acute events
Engage in moderate exercise when eosinophilia is well-controlled — exercise is beneficial for overall cardiovascular health; avoid overexertion during active eosinophilic organ involvement

recommended: Anti-inflammatory diet (Mediterranean diet pattern) — omega-3 rich foods (fatty fish, walnuts, flaxseed) may have modest eosinophil-stabilizing properties; reduces overall inflammatory burden
High-protein diet — maintains muscle mass and immune function; relevant for patients with chronic eosinophilic disease and those on corticosteroids
Vitamin D 1000-2000 IU daily — vitamin D has immune-modulating properties; low vitamin D is associated with more severe allergic disease and asthma
avoid: Food triggers in patients with known EoE — milk, wheat, egg, soy, and peanuts are the most common food triggers for EoE; food elimination diets (particularly the 6-food elimination diet) are an established treatment for EoE

Raw fish in patients with known parasitic eosinophilia or from endemic Strongyloides regions — raw fish can harbor parasites that cause eosinophilic infection (Anisakis from raw fish; Gnathostoma from raw freshwater fish)
Excessive alcohol — alcohol can exacerbate eosinophilic GI symptoms; alcohol-related liver disease also impairs eosinophil clearance

exerciseGuidelines: Exercise is generally safe and beneficial for patients with eosinophilia when the condition is well-controlled (AEC below 1,500 cells/mcL, no active organ involvement). Aerobic exercise 150 minutes per week is recommended. During active eosinophilic myocarditis or neuropathy, strenuous exercise is contraindicated until cardiac and neurological status are cleared by the treating physician. Patients on high-dose corticosteroids should follow bone safety guidelines (weight-bearing exercise to prevent osteoporosis).
dailyManagement: Monitor AEC according to the schedule set by your hematologist — typically monthly during active treatment, then every 3-6 months in remission; report any sudden rise in AEC (above 1,500 cells/mcL) to your care team immediately
Take medications as prescribed — corticosteroids, mepolizumab, imatinib, or antihelminthics; do not skip doses or stop corticosteroids without medical supervision (can cause adrenal crisis)
Report any new symptoms promptly — rash, fever, chest pain, palpitations, dysphagia, numbness, tingling, or weakness; early reporting of organ involvement allows timely intervention

Avoid all drugs associated with DRESS — keep a list of contraindicated drugs (allopurinol, aromatic anticonvulsants, sulfonamides) and show it to every healthcare provider before any new medication is prescribed
Obtain an echocardiogram as recommended — cardiac involvement in HES can be silent until advanced; your physician will determine the appropriate monitoring interval based on your AEC and risk factors
prognosis: The prognosis of eosinophilia depends entirely on the underlying cause. Secondary eosinophilia (allergic, parasitic) has an excellent prognosis — parasitic eosinophilia is cured with specific antihelminthic therapy, and allergic eosinophilia is managed by controlling the underlying allergy. DRESS syndrome has a mortality of 10-20% without treatment but drops to approximately 5% with prompt corticosteroid therapy. HES has a more variable prognosis — PDGFR+ HES has a normal life expectancy with imatinib, while untreated HES with cardiac involvement has a 5-year survival of approximately 50%. Early identification and treatment of cardiac and neurological complications is the primary determinant of outcome. Modern therapies (corticosteroids, mepolizumab, imatinib) have substantially improved survival and quality of life in HES.
recoveryTimeline: Secondary eosinophilia from parasites normalizes within 2-4 weeks of successful antihelminthic therapy — the eosinophil count typically falls progressively over this period. DRESS syndrome requires 6-12 months of corticosteroid therapy for full resolution of organ involvement; skin findings may take months to fully resolve. HES managed with corticosteroids shows AEC normalization within days to weeks, but organ recovery (particularly cardiac and neurological) is slower and may be incomplete if damage was advanced before treatment started. Mepolizumab and imatinib responses are rapid (weeks) and durable (years).
complications: Eosinophilic myocarditis and endomyocardial fibrosis — the most serious complication of HES; eosinophil degranulation in the myocardium causes necrosis, fibrosis, and thrombosis; presents with chest pain, heart failure, and arrhythmias; can be fatal without treatment; echocardiogram is essential for early detection

Eosinophilic neuropathy — mononeuritis multiplex affecting peripheral nerves; causes asymmetric sensory and motor deficits; early treatment prevents permanent nerve damage; nerve conduction studies confirm the pattern

Eosinophilic thromboembolism — HES patients are hypercoagulable due to eosinophil-derived tissue factor; stroke, DVT, PE, and arterial thromboses are major causes of morbidity and mortality; anticoagulation may be required
DRESS syndrome with organ failure — severe DRESS causes hepatic necrosis, renal failure, and cardiac involvement; mortality of 10-20% without treatment

Eosinophilic gastroenteritis with protein-losing enteropathy — severe eosinophilic infiltration of the GI tract causes protein loss through the mucosa, leading to hypoalbuminemia, peripheral edema, and ascites

Eosinophilic esophagitis with stricture formation — chronic esophageal eosinophilia causes fibrosis and stricturing; progressive dysphagia and risk of food impaction are the primary complications; endoscopic dilation may be needed

supportResources.name: The Hypereosinophilic Syndrome Foundation
supportResources.description: Patient advocacy organization for HES — provides education, support groups, research updates, and clinical trial information.

supportResources.name: American Partnership for Eosinophilic Disorders (APFED)
supportResources.description: Patient organization for eosinophilic disorders — covers EoE, EG, and other eosinophilic GI diseases; provides resources for patients and families.

supportResources.name: WHO Classification of Tumours of Haematopoietic and Lymphoid Tissues (2022 revision) — CEL-NOS Chapter
supportResources.description: The WHO/ICC classification of eosinophilic neoplasms — defines chronic eosinophilic leukemia, HES subtypes, and diagnostic criteria.

supportResources.name: Klion AD. How to treat hypereosinophilic syndromes. Nat Rev Immunol. 2021.
supportResources.description: Expert review on the diagnosis, classification, and treatment of HES — the definitive clinical reference for managing eosinophilic disorders.

supportResources.name: Bosisio D, et al. Eosinophils in health and disease. Nat Rev Immunol. 2023.
supportResources.description: Comprehensive review of eosinophil biology — essential background for understanding the mechanisms underlying eosinophilic disease.

supportResources.name: Ogbogu PU, et al. Hypereosinophilic syndrome: An update. Blood. 2022.
supportResources.description: Updated review of HES epidemiology, classification, and management strategies with emphasis on targeted therapies.

confusedWithConditions.name: Asthma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Asthma causes airway eosinophilia and can cause mild peripheral eosinophilia (usually below 1,500 cells/mcL), but the peripheral eosinophilia of asthma alone does not cause the systemic complications of HES. Patients with asthma and marked eosinophilia may actually have EGPA — the asthma of EGPA typically has a late-onset pattern.

confusedWithConditions.name: Eosinophilic Granulomatosis with Polyangiitis (EGPA/Churg-Strauss)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: EGPA is a vasculitis defined by the triad of asthma, sinusitis, and eosinophilia, but it adds a systemic vasculitic component affecting nerves, skin, heart, and kidneys. The vasculitic features (not just eosinophilia) distinguish EGPA from benign allergic eosinophilia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Eosinophilic Esophagitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: EoE causes esophageal eosinophilia with dysphagia and food impaction but peripheral eosinophilia may be normal or only mildly elevated. The distinction from HES is that EoE is organ-restricted with no end-organ damage beyond the esophagus.

confusedWithConditions.name: Chronic Myeloid Leukemia (CML)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: CML can cause eosinophilia as a presenting feature, but CML has a distinctive peripheral smear with left shift (immature myeloid cells), basophilia, and splenomegaly. BCR-ABL testing distinguishes CML from HES. Eosinophilia alone (without left shift or basophilia) is more consistent with HES.

relatedConditions.name: EGPA (Churg-Strauss)
relatedConditions.relevance: EGPA is the most clinically important vasculitic cause of eosinophilia — it combines allergy/asthma, eosinophilia, and systemic vasculitis. All patients with late-onset asthma and eosinophilia should be screened for EGPA with ANCA testing and neurological assessment.

relatedConditions.name: Eosinophilic Esophagitis
relatedConditions.relevance: EoE is the most common organ-restricted eosinophilic disorder; it is managed by gastroenterologists and allergists, often without hematology involvement. EoE patients should be monitored for systemic eosinophilia and HES.

relatedConditions.name: Asthma
relatedConditions.relevance: Eosinophilic asthma (a Th2-high asthma phenotype) shares the mechanism of IL-5-mediated eosinophil activation with HES; mepolizumab (anti-IL-5) was first developed for severe eosinophilic asthma before being approved for EGPA and studied in HES.

relatedConditions.name: Pernicious Anemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Both pernicious anemia and eosinophilic conditions can be associated with autoimmune polyendocrinopathy; autoimmune thyroid disease, pernicious anemia, and eosinophilic disorders can coexist in the context of immune dysregulation.

faqs.question: What is eosinophilia?
faqs.answer: Eosinophilia is an elevation in the eosinophil count above 500 cells per microliter of blood. Eosinophils are white blood cells that fight parasites and modulate allergic reactions. Eosinophilia is a sign of an underlying problem — most commonly allergies, parasitic infections, or in severe cases, hypereosinophilic syndrome (HES).

faqs.question: What causes eosinophilia?
faqs.answer: The most common causes are allergies (asthma, drug reactions, skin disease), helminthic parasites (hookworm, Strongyloides, Ascaris), drug reactions (DRESS syndrome), and hypereosinophilic syndrome (HES). Rare causes include adrenal insufficiency, some cancers, and eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis (EGPA).

faqs.question: What is a dangerously high eosinophil count?
faqs.answer: An absolute eosinophil count above 1,500 cells/mcL that persists for more than 6 months — especially with symptoms — is the threshold for hypereosinophilic syndrome (HES) and carries risk of organ damage to the heart, nerves, and GI tract.

faqs.question: How is eosinophilia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis starts with a complete blood count confirming the elevated AEC. Mild eosinophilia with an obvious allergic trigger may require no further testing. Persistent moderate-to-marked eosinophilia requires stool parasite testing, Strongyloides serology, echocardiogram, ANCA testing, serum tryptase, and bone marrow with FIP1L1-PDGFRA testing.

faqs.question: What is hypereosinophilic syndrome (HES)?
faqs.answer: HES is defined as an AEC above 1,500 cells/mcL persisting longer than 6 months with end-organ damage and no identifiable secondary cause. It is classified into PDGFR+ HES (responds to imatinib), lymphoid HES (responds to anti-IL-5), and idiopathic HES (diagnosis of exclusion).

faqs.question: What is the treatment for eosinophilia?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on the cause: antihistamines and corticosteroids for allergic eosinophilia; albendazole or ivermectin for parasitic eosinophilia; corticosteroids for HES; imatinib for PDGFR+ HES; mepolizumab for EGPA and steroid-sparing in HES. Treating the underlying cause is always the first step.

faqs.question: Can parasites cause high eosinophilia?
faqs.answer: Yes — tissue-invasive helminthic parasites are the most common cause of marked eosinophilia in tropical regions. Strongyloides stercoralis, hookworm, Ascaris, and filarial worms all cause eosinophilia that can exceed 5,000 cells/mcL. Albendazole or ivermectin clears the infection and resolves the eosinophilia.

faqs.question: Is eosinophilia a sign of cancer?
faqs.answer: Eosinophilia is rarely the presenting sign of cancer. Some solid tumors (particularly lung, stomach, and cervical cancers) and hematologic malignancies (lymphomas, CML) can produce paraneoplastic eosinophilia — but this is uncommon. Most eosinophilia is due to allergies, parasites, or HES, not cancer.

faqs.question: What is DRESS syndrome?
faqs.answer: DRESS (Drug Reaction with Eosinophilia and Systemic Symptoms) is a severe, potentially fatal drug reaction. Common culprits are aromatic anticonvulsants, allopurinol, sulfonamides, and vancomycin. It presents with rash, fever, lymphadenopathy, hepatitis, and marked eosinophilia 2-8 weeks after starting a new drug. Immediate drug discontinuation and corticosteroids are required.

faqs.question: What organs does eosinophilia damage?
faqs.answer: The heart (eosinophilic myocarditis, endomyocardial fibrosis), nervous system (peripheral neuropathy, mononeuritis multiplex), and GI tract (eosinophilic esophagitis, gastroenteritis) are the primary end-organ targets of HES. The skin (urticaria, angioedema) and lungs are commonly affected in secondary eosinophilia.

faqs.question: What is imatinib used for in eosinophilia?
faqs.answer: Imatinib is used for FIP1L1-PDGFRA+ HES — the most common PDGFR+ HES variant. The FIP1L1-PDGFRA fusion protein is a constitutively active tyrosine kinase; imatinib inhibits this kinase and produces complete remission in over 95% of patients within weeks.

faqs.question: How is Strongyloides stercoralis diagnosed and treated?
faqs.answer: Strongyloides is diagnosed by stool microscopy (three samples), agar plate culture, or serology. Treatment is ivermectin (single dose, repeated at 2 weeks) or albendazole. Strongyloides is important because it can persist for decades through autoinfection and causes fatal hyperinfection syndrome in immunocompromised hosts.

faqs.question: What is the difference between EGPA and HES?
faqs.answer: EGPA (eosinophilic granulomatosis with polyangiitis, formerly Churg-Strauss) is a vasculitis characterized by asthma, sinusitis, eosinophilia, and small vessel vasculitis. HES is defined by marked persistent eosinophilia with end-organ damage but without the systemic vasculitic features of EGPA. Both have eosinophilia but are pathophysiologically distinct.

faqs.question: Can stress cause eosinophilia?
faqs.answer: Adrenal insufficiency (Addison disease) causes eosinophilia because cortisol normally suppresses eosinophils. In adrenal crisis, eosinophilia with relative lymphopenia is a classic finding. In clinical practice, most eosinophilia is not caused by psychological stress but by allergies, parasites, or drugs.

faqs.question: Is eosinophilia curable?
faqs.answer: Secondary eosinophilia from parasites or allergies is curable or controllable when the underlying cause is treated — albendazole/ivermectin cure helminth infections, allergen avoidance controls allergic eosinophilia. HES is manageable but not curable; lifelong treatment and monitoring is required to prevent organ damage.

faqs.question: What is mepolizumab used for in eosinophilia?
faqs.answer: Mepolizumab (anti-IL-5) is a monoclonal antibody that binds IL-5, preventing it from activating eosinophils. It is FDA-approved for severe eosinophilic asthma and EGPA, and used off-label for HES. It reduces eosinophil counts, prevents exacerbations, and allows corticosteroid dose reduction.

faqs.question: What is FIP1L1-PDGFRA?
faqs.answer: FIP1L1-PDGFRA is a fusion gene created by a chromosomal deletion that fuses FIP1L1 (on chromosome 4) to PDGFRA (on chromosome 4). The resulting fusion protein is a constitutively active tyrosine kinase that drives eosinophil overproduction in PDGFR+ HES. It is detected by FISH or PCR and makes the patient eligible for imatinib therapy.

faqs.question: How long does eosinophilia last after parasitic infection treatment?
faqs.answer: After successful antihelminthic therapy (albendazole or ivermectin), the peripheral eosinophil count typically normalizes within 2-4 weeks. Follicular eosinophilia from allergic conditions normalizes when the allergic trigger is controlled — this may take weeks to months depending on the allergen and treatment.
